speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November the 30th, 1995. The survivor is Mr. Mendel Tessler-- T-E-S-S-L-E-R, interviewer Felicia Carmelly-- C-A-R-M-E-L-L-Y. We are in Thornhill, Ontario, Canada. This interview is in English. Hello, my name is Felicia Carmelly. I will be interviewing today Mr. Mendel Tessler. We are in Toronto-- Thornhill, Ontario, Canada. And this interview is in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Mendel Tessler. In Canada, they called me Menley. And in Russia, they called me Misha-- Misha [RUSSIAN] because I was a truck driver and was a bus driver.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [RUSSIAN] is the word for the driver--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: --in Russian. Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Korzec, [NON-ENGLISH]. This is the province from Rivne.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: July the 23rd, 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aba.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aba Tessler?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was manufacturing combs.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, and your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name was what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tarach.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and was she a house wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she was many things-- after my father died, she was still going on with the little factory. But then she gave up. And she was doing housework, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. How many children were in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were five.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us about them please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had two sisters and two brothers. The youngest brother died, I think about 1921, after my father died.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Idle. And he died from the same sickness like my father, from typhus. And before my father died, we lived in that little town. And as you know, typhus is a very-- with a lot of temperature.

speaker: interviewer
text: So somehow he got a little better. And he said, how can I die while my neighbor next door have woman with two daughters haven't got enough wood to pull through the winter? Because the winters were very cold. So he got a little better. He went out in the market. And he bought a wagon with wood. And he send over to the poor family so they should live through. And that's what they did. OK, so tell me about your other brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I had-- older sister of was Faiga. My other sister was Zlata. She's still alive. And she's here in Toronto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the girls married during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. No, no. The girls were married before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And my brother was killed when the Germans came in the first day. And his name--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shlomo.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shlomo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was killed when the Germans came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first day when they came in-- I think July the 3rd or the 4th.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was he killed? They took 350 men what they called them then they're educated, Politische, you know. He didn't have nothing to do with politic, because he was a tailor and didn't know anything about politic. But they picked out 350 men. They took them out of town on the open field. And they killed them. And did you tell us the names of the other two sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Faiga--

speaker: interviewer
text: And one is Faiga--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Zlata.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Zlata.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right-- Shlomo--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they lived with their own families.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right

speaker: interviewer
text: Also in Korzec.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Korzec.

speaker: interviewer
text: Korzec. And they had children?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had two children, each one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Each one had two children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and they were slaughtered, the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: With their husbands and the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the husbands run away. But Zlata's husband was killed in 1945 from the partisans. And my older sister's-- Faiga-- her husband is now in Israel, is alive. But the children were left someplace in a field. And nobody knows where they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were they left in the field?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-in-law, my older sister's husband, gave them over to a Ukrainian family. But this Ukrainian was working for my mother manufacturing the combs. And he promised them he will keep them. And as soon as he left the children, the children disappeared. And nobody knows as of today where they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, so what do you remember from your childhood? How was it for you to be a little boy in Korzec in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I lost my father when I was four to five years old. And my mother never remarried. So I looked for work-- anything to do. If I could find work for a day and to make a dollar, for the whole week, I was a very happy man. But then I'd decided I want to be a bus driver. Because in our town was only one bus coming in from the province, from Rivne, and I was dreamed to be a bus driver. So I was working two years without a penny as a helper for the bus driver. And then I got my license. And when got my license, I couldn't believe that a little man like me-- a little Jew like me-- can drive a bus of 30, 40 people. Because we were only two Jewish bus drivers in the whole province. The other guy was killed, and I'm alive. But all my dream was to help out my mother, because she was widow. She never remarried. We had chances to go away to United States, because I had a grandfather in New York. But she says no. Either we go all of the four of us or nobody.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grandfather's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Michel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did Michel get to New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: He left Korzec in the '30s-- 1932, 1933. They were struggling in New York. And he used to send us in sometimes $5, $10. And that's the way my mother made a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was your mother's father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what his last name-- he was Michel what?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was Michel Tarach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tarach, right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they change the name to Glazer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and what about your father's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were in Zhytomyr.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Zhytomyr?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was also in Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no-- Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was in the Ukraine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. So what else do you remember from your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember lots of things. But if I will tell you the history, so I don't think you'll have enough tape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll tell you major things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me some major things that you remember, things that really influenced your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, major things was then we were living in a little town surrounded with Ukrainians-- murderers, gangsters, you know-- it was scary to go out on the street. And we lived--

speaker: interviewer
text: What would they do to you when you went out on the street?

speaker: interviewee
text: They would cut off your ear. They would smash you, you know. Always were some incidents. So we tried to stay at home. But the problem was this-- then we were on the border of Russia and Poland up until 1939. In 1939, the Russian came. And they divided Poland on two parts-- one part up to Bug, and the other part is Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other part was German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was German, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the life was always scary. On top of this, we couldn't go out on the street after 7 o'clock, because the town was on the border Russia and Poland. We never knew what goes on in Russia. It was a iron curtain-- we never met anybody. We couldn't even look on the Russian side. On the outskirt of the town was Red Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Bug divided the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not the Bug-- no?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the Bug divided the country, I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Bug divided the country, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right. Do you remember any nice things from home? Like how did you celebrate holidays? What were some nice things about--

speaker: interviewee
text: We did not celebrate the holidays, because my mother, when she lost her husband, she was always in big mournings. You know, she was always crying. We never sat seder. Sure, we did belong to the shul where my late father used to belong. But we didn't-- I mean, we knew about the holidays, but we didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: Have a celebration.

speaker: interviewee
text: --celebration-- no, no, no. But it's only because the mother was always sitting and crying, and crying, crying.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So did your other brother also help out for the house before he was shot?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, yeah. He became a schneider-- a tailor, yes. And then my sister became a dressmaker. So we all the four were working so it would be easier for my mother, you know, to buy shoes and to buy clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what do you remember about the changes in Korzec before the war started? What were the changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1939, the Russians came into our town. Of course, everybody was scared to go out on the street. But the Russians told us, don't worry. We didn't come to kill you. We just came to free you from the Polish regime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were working for two years with the Russians together. In 1941, the war broke out. And then we saw the big disaster. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you run? What was wrong that you have to run?

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason that I did run away-- I was a truck driver for the government of Russia. And I was working for the MTS-- [RUSSIAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: [RUSSIAN] is a factory which makes machinery and tractors.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, right, right. The chairman was a member of the party. So the war broke out June the 22nd, 4 o'clock at night. And within a week, Italy was already close to our town. I was working a truck as a truck driver. And there was no vehicles, because all the vehicles they took away to the front. But because I was working for the government, they left that vehicle in town.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the chairman insisted I should take him away to the railway. And the railway was 29 kilometers away from our town. So I came over with the truck to my house. And I begged the family, please come with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was not transportation. There was even no horses. But I had a chance to take everybody with me. And my brother says, as soon as we leave the house, we'll be poor people. I remember that I told them, what's more important-- to be poor or to save life. I couldn't make them going. So I grabbed my mother. And I put her in on the truck. And I left town. And the first railway was 29 kilometers in Novograd Volynsk. So on the road, the army stopped me. And they said, I must go to the army. I said, I must take the chairman to the railway. And somehow, I came to the railway. And mother saw what goes on the highways. We saw these German soldiers were dressed with Russian clothes, but they couldn't speak Russian. They were thrown down from the planes as parachutes. And the Russians got them. They took them on the field. And I don't know what even the end of them. But my mother saw what goes on. So she decided she'll walk back home. And all of a sudden, I see my mother disappeared. So I left the truck in the field after when I let down the chairman from the party. And I left the truck. And I went back home. This was 29 kilometers. So it took me a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't go by truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you go by truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the tires gave up. So I came back home. And I begged my mother she should come with me. And she says no. Whatever will happen with everybody will happen with me too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I had a bicycle. So I grabbed the bicycle. And as I came out and standing in front of the house, a power, a [NON-ENGLISH], you know, pushed me in the back and says go away, go away, go away. I don't believe. I'm not a big believer, especially after this Holocaust what's happened. But some other power pushed me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I grabbed the bicycle. And I ran any. And I came to that Novograd Volynsk. I dropped the bicycle, and I jumped on a freight train. And for 10 days and 10 nights, I went to Kiev, and to Rostov, to Stalingrad-- farther, farther, farther away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just ask you a question. This happened in 1941, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you already know then what's happening in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't know. In 1940--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were people so afraid?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1939, to our town came refugees-- dentists, doctors. And they told us what goes on--

speaker: interviewer
text: From--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Germany. And they told us what goes on, that the Germans are killing Jews. I said, why? For what reason? I am not afraid of them. I'm a worker.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm not a politician. I don't know anything about politic. I don't care who's the government. But I never could believe then that gangster will take innocent people-- shoemakers, and carpenters-- and kill them, for what? My mind couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: In our house was a family from Germany. They told us that they are grabbing the Jews-- what's happened the Crystal Night. But we still didn't believe them until we saw what goes on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you ended up to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: I ended up-- I landed up in Stalingrad. From Stalingrad, I went to Rostov.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to you in Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working. Right away when I came to a little village, there was no truck drivers because all the men went away to the front. They didn't take me to the front, because I wasn't a Russian citizen. I was a Polish citizen, so they let me stay. Otherwise I would be in the front with other people. But now I want to tell you that in our town, when the Russian came, they divided-- the had two-- [NON-ENGLISH]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Conscriptions.

speaker: interviewee
text: One group of people they send on the front to the west. Nobody came alive. Another group they sent away to the east up till Siberia, Chelyabinsk-- those people, they were all saved. So I reached this Chelyabinsk too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the Stalingrad, you worked as a as a bus--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was working all over as a truck driver.

speaker: interviewer
text: So why did you leave Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the Germans were already two kilometers from Stalingrad. And the fights were from house to house. So I went to Rostov. Then they came closer to Rostov. So I went away to Siberia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to Chelyabinsk.

speaker: interviewee
text: Chelyabinsk, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Truck driver.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was life there for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible. I mean, for me-- life for me was easy on account of my trade. Any place they came, they were just grabbing me. They were happy to have me there to do some work, because there was no man. There was no truck drivers. So thanks to my profession, you know, I am alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you just said before that life for other people was terrible?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was terrible?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cold-- 50 below and 60 below. People were working on the street with no clothes-- I mean, with no heavy clothes. They were working-- they put in a sewer in the ground with picks-- you know, pick and a hammer, you know? It was no food, no heat, no houses. So anybody who was strong, they lived through. And the other ones, most of them they died from starvation.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was in Chelyabinsk before the war? Were people not living there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, for sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they lived in these terrible conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But that time before the war was different. Everybody had a house. Everybody had a place where to stay. They were work for the government. But these poor refugees would run away from Poland, from Ukraine, you know-- they'd have nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, but the local people in Chelyabinsk had it better?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how long were you in Chelyabinsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was for about a year. Then I heard then Sonia, my wife, is someplace in Tashkent.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't married then, were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. In that time, when we heard that someplace is a human being from my town, right away we try to help them, especially me. I had money. And I had a chance to take the train. Because you have to bribe the conductor to get a ticket.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I took the train for 10 days and 10 nights. I knew Sonia from home, because we went to school together. I knew her when she was seven years old. I was older, because I went to the yeshiva for three years in Korzec. So when I find out that she's there, I took the train. And I took with me five liter of vodka. Because I knew that with vodka, I can do anything-- and traveling for 10 days. And I came to a place-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And I saw Sonia. In Tashkent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Close to Tashkent. Yangiyul. When I came in, she was on a clay floor lying and threshing rice. You know what threshing means?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's rice. And the rice has to come out from the shell. So she was threshing this thing. I had with me 88,000 rubles-- 44,000 rubles in one boot and 44,000 rubles in another boot. I took Sonia on the market. And on the market, you can get anything-- anything what you have desires. But the price was very, very high. I dressed her complete. And I didn't come with anything in my mind, you know, that I'll marry her or sex, or something. Because that time, was nothing in the mind, but just to help somebody out-- especially I knew she was a delicate person at home. We went to school together for a few years.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then I says, do you want to stay here, or do you want to come with me to Chelyabinsk? I do not want that people should think that I came to use her. She says, well, OK, I'll go.

speaker: interviewee
text: And as soon as I came to Chelyabinsk, right away they call me to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: They called you to the army.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah. So I changed my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how come they called you to the army now? You were still a Polish citizen.

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, it was luck too. Some of them they let through, and some of them they took to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a disorder. Anyways, I got-- what they call this thing-- [NON-ENGLISH]. They called me to the army. And I have to come. Here is Sonia with me. I took away from the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So I changed my name. And I ran away with Sonia back to Tashkent. How did you manage to change your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just told them that my name is Konstantinowsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, but you needed papers for--

speaker: interviewee
text: What can papers? What papers? They believed-- papers, I destroyed the papers. It wasn't a order like now.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Russia, everybody was supposed to have an identity, otherwise--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, I destroyed it. I destroyed my license and my passport when I left home too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, when I came to Stalingrad, they believed me. They gave me a license.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I came to Chelyabinsk, and I says, I run away from Poland. But I have no passport. What's your name? Konstantinowsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took me as Konstantinowsky. From now on, I am Konstantinowsky. Then when I came Tashkent, I change my name again to Tessler. I see. So how long were you in Tashkent?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tashkent, we were until 1945. We got married in October, 1943. The first daughter was born in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Tashkent--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tashkent, right. And then the war was still in Berlin. We decided we'll go to Poland to see-- we never heard what goes on in Poland. We never knew what goes on in Poland. We only heard one thing then in our town, the Germans slaughtered 105 citizens. In Russia, there was no nationalities, but citizen-- [NON-ENGLISH] is citizens. But we never knew what they did with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you hear that-- that they killed the--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the radio. It was on the radio once. But otherwise, we never, never-- until I came to Poland in 1945, where we landed in Krakow. And then we went to Bytom. We heard about Auschwitz, about Dachau, about the terrible things what's happen. Then my sister ran away too. But she has a bigger history than we. Because she was led to the grave with two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just a moment-- when was this when she ran away?

speaker: interviewee
text: She ran away 1942.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which sister was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Selma-- Zlata.

speaker: interviewer
text: Zlata.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: She ran away?

speaker: interviewee
text: She ran away. She dropped the two children. I don't know, somehow-- she'll tell you. I hope that you'll be able to take from her. But she's a--

speaker: interviewer
text: She's in Toronto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But you can't talk to her. She's destroyed all her life, you know. But if you'll have a chance, maybe, she can tell you the horrible, horrible things what she lived through.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will certainly try.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians chased out the Germans from our town, she was that time in the Czech army. Because she was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was working in [NON-ENGLISH]-- [NON-ENGLISH] is in Russia, you know-- as a Polish citizen. But then somehow, the chairman from the factory told her, you know then that the Gestapo is looking for you. She run away. And as she run away, the Czech army came to [NON-ENGLISH]. First, they chased out the Germans. So she knew that I'm alive someplace. So she jumped in a truck. And she came to Korzec. And she met a friend of mine what he was my place in Tashkent a few days ago. Since then--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did he get back from Tashkent to Korzec in the middle of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the war was that time in Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: But your sister ran away, you said, in 1942.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she ran away from the Germans to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But my friend, what he met my sister, this was already when Korzec was free from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was in the Czech army. So she got permission to go to little town of Korzec and to find out if I'm alive. And accidentally, she met a friend. But he came from Tashkent going to Poland. He told her that I'm alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you two meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Prague, Czechoslovakia. We were in Bytom when the war was still going on in Berlin. And we had a phone call from my sister, because she married a major from the Czech army. He was Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she find out that I'm in Bytom, so she called us. So then we grabbed a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. You know what a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] is. A convoy of trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Trucks-- a train, actually. So we came to the station. And the time it was at--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: --at that time was a transport from the Romanian Jews going back from Auschwitz from Poland to Romania. So we jumped on the train as Romanian Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we need to stop here. We'll continue on the next tape. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
